@rbarlow there's no point in rehashing the GPL vs permissive debate. In part because Python packaging is such a disaster zone, it has become quite common in enterprise settings to distribute relocatable Python environments, and concerns around distributing GPL dependencies frequently wastes user time. Developers are free to choose this license, but it is often easier for users to drop the GPL code altogether than to have the lawyers analyze the way in which the software is used to determine if the viral provisions of the GPL "kick in".

The current implementation assumes all extensions, unless `EXTENSIONS` with a list of extensions followingis is passed. If the list contains a `NONE` extension, it considers that zero extensions are required. This solution is not perfect but allows all possibilities:
- all extensions: no `EXTENSIONS` or empty `EXTENSIONS` list;
- no extensions: `EXTENSIONS NONE`; and
- some extensions: `EXTENSIONS EXT1 EXT2`

Something like
```python
iaa.Sequential(
    ...
    iaa.Sometimes(0.5, iaa.GaussianBlur((0.1, 0.3))

)
```
would mean, that only in 50% of all cases gaussian blur with a random strength between 0.1 and 0.3 is applied. So basically 50% of all images are not affected by any form of gaussian blur. The Sequential simply skips that augmenter completely in these cases, as if it didn't contain it.

Ok, I felt really bad by neglecting these issues, and every time that I thought about it, that idea pushed me away from going back to work on pew once again :/

Now I finally booted into windows to dig into this issue.

Good news: pew has worked inside `babun` all along.

Bad news: this applies only to a python installed inside cygwin.

This is the [`CreateProcess`](https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/windows/desktop/ms682425%28v=vs.85%29.aspx?f=255&MSPPError=-2147217396) I was talking about. Python's [subprocess](https://docs.python.org/3/library/subprocess.html) uses `popen` on posix systems, and `CreateProcess` on windows. So what I meant earlier is that the error was due to a `subprocess.call` or `subprocess.check_call`, and the error message was Windows specific.

Basically, the problem you've found is because `SHELL` is set to `/bin/zsh`, but `CreateProcess` is unable to find `/bin/zsh` (obviously, since that's not a valid Windows path)

You can work around it with `env SHELL=zsh pew workon envname` or `pew in envname zsh`

But then you'll stumble another error... pew will use `sys.executable` (that is the path of the Python interpreter itself). The problem now is the opposite: this is a Windows path and it'll be supplied as an argument to `SHELL` (zsh in this case) which doesn't treat `\` as the file separator.

I tried to make a simple conversion with `Path(sys.executable).as_posix()` but this will yield `C:/yada/yada` which won't still be accepted by zsh due to the leading drive specifier.

You can see the difference in behavior between the 2 pythons here:

    { ~ }  » /usr/bin/python                                                                        ~
    Python 2.7.8 (default, Jul 28 2014, 01:34:03)
    [GCC 4.8.3] on cygwin
    Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
    >>> import os
    >>> os.path.sep
    '/'
    >>> from subprocess import call
    >>> call(['/bin/zsh', '-c', 'echo 1'])
    1
    0

Looking at the GRE issue, during debugging I found out that code:

    # XXX - auto-load GRE dispatch table from Ethernet dispatch table
    from . import ethernet
    GRE._protosw.update(ethernet.Ethernet._typesw)

from ``gre.py`` is actually called multiple time and the first call returns: ``cannot import name ethernet``.

I looked to it and found out that although in ``__init__.py`` the ``ethernet`` module is imported before ``gre``:

    from . import ethernet
    from . import gre

That in fact the import of ``ethernet`` module leads into import of ``gre`` which then tries to import ``ethernet`` which isn't fully loaded yet (another circular dependency).

The call stack is following:

1) ``import dpkt`` is called from an external code (reproducer in my case)
2) ``__init__.py`` calls ``from . import ethernet`` on line ``27``
3) ``ethernet.py`` calls ``__load_types()`` on line ``254``, the function is in the same module and causes that ``ip6.py`` module is imported.
4) ``ip6.py`` imports ``ip`` module on line ``146``
5) ``ip.py`` calls ``__load_protos()`` on line ``362``, the function is in the same file (``ip.py``)
6) the ``__load_protos()`` imports modules corresponding to constants with ``IP_PROTO_`` in ``ip.py`` file. The ``gre`` module is there too.

Thanks to this, ``gre`` module is actually being imported from ``ip`` module during initialization of ``ethernet`` module. But ``gre`` needs ``ethernet`` module (which is not ready yet), thus the first initialization (the one called from ``ip``) fails. In the end, ``gre`` is fully initialized once it's directly imported from ``__init__.py``, but in that time, ``ip`` is already loaded and misses support for ``gre`` (because when ``ip`` was loading ``gre`` it failed).

@obormot, @brifordwylie 
Thinking about that, it maybe worth it to actually call ``__load_protos()`` and similar functions during first instantiation of ``IP``, ``IP6``, etc. classes (i.e. in time when all modules are most likely already initialized) and not during module initialization.

History in `bash` afterward:

I'm using the builtin gc module.

Here is a piece of code that outputs the objects caught up in the reference cycles. After clearing the exception history (which should clean up all the objects if it weren't for the cycles), a manual garbage collectoin is triggered by gc.collect(). After that, gc.garbage contains the objects that are unreachable but not collected.

Please note that this code uses DEBUG_SAVEALL, which marks every unreachable reference cycle as garbage. This does not necessary mean leakage, as reference cycles can be gc-d if they do not involve any objects with __del__ methods. If you change it to DEBUG_UNCOLLECTABLE, it only gathers uncollectable objects (permanent leaks), which is an empty list for this test case (because it does not involve any objects with __del__).

In a more involved case, I can produce uncollectable cycles too, with demonstrable fd leaks, but apparently it needs SSL with validated certificates (that fail in very specific kind of ways), it would need a more complex test code to reproduce that. I assume that the ssl module has some uncollectable objects that get caught up in the reference cycles, resulting in the fd leaks.

```import socketserver
import threading
from ldap3 import Server, Connection, RESTARTABLE, set_config_parameter, ServerPool

import gc, pprint
gc.set_debug(gc.DEBUG_SAVEALL)

set_config_parameter("RESTARTABLE_TRIES", 2) # number of attemps
set_config_parameter("POOLING_LOOP_TIMEOUT", 1) # sleep between attempts

#dummy server that accepts a connection and closes it immediately
sock_server = socketserver.TCPServer(("127.0.0.1", 0), socketserver.BaseRequestHandler)
threading.Thread(target=sock_server.serve_forever).start()

server = Server(sock_server.server_address[0], sock_server.server_address[1], use_ssl=False)
connection = Connection(ServerPool([server], active=1), client_strategy=RESTARTABLE)

try:
    connection.bind()
except Exception as e:
    print("Exception was caught: ", e)

sock_server.shutdown()

connection.strategy._reset_exception_history()
gc.collect()
pprint.pprint(gc.garbage, indent=4)```

Hi, to set the timeout while establishing the connection you must set the ``connect_timeout`` parameter of the server object. The ``receive_timeout`` of the Connection object is used only when the connection is open and ldap3 is awaiting for a response from the server.  

Also, if you are using the RESTARTABLE or REUSABLE strategies the operation is retried for the number of times specified in the RESTARTABLE_TRIES global parameter, that is set to 30 by default. You can change it with the following code:

```
from ldap3 import set_config_parameter
# set number of tries for the RESTARTABLE and REUSABLE strategies
set_config_parameter('RESTARTABLE_TRIES', 5)
```

Your first link deals with creating expensive logging messages which is not necessarily a fault of the logging module. But it is quite common and has an easy fix: [logging.Logger.isEnabledFor](https://docs.python.org/2/howto/logging.html#optimization)

```
if logger.isEnabledFor(logging.DEBUG):
    logger.debug('Message with %s, expensive_func())
```

That does bring up a good point though. It would be nice to add an option that will do this for you automatically.

The other link about tight loops is similarly answered:

> Note
> 
> In some cases, isEnabledFor() can itself be more expensive than you’d like (e.g. for deeply nested loggers where an explicit level is only set high up in the logger hierarchy). In such cases **(or if you want to avoid calling a method in tight loops)**, you can cache the result of a call to isEnabledFor() in a local or instance variable, and use that instead of calling the method each time. Such a cached value would only need to be recomputed when the logging configuration changes dynamically while the application is running (which is not all that common).

I agree with @nawagers that it depends on the goals and I think the goals he proposes sound worthwile. For me the most important goals from his list are 1 and 4: The library should be idempotent in the sense that it should _allow to losslesly read and write back out any well-formed gpx_.

At first glance, writing the implementation of additional extension classes in the style @tkrajina is suggesting would be the most consistent way of implementing a new extension. If the extension schema is known, it can be implemented and writing it like @tkrajina suggests would allow to write back consistent XML. But one encounters the following problems:

**The tags in extensions live in different namespaces. Currently gpxpy is not namespace-aware.**
Without extensions, caring about namespace was not important because the whole file would be in the GPX namespace. This complicates implementation because different extensions can define the same tags, possibly with different meanings or containing different data types (e.g. cadence as float vs cadence as int). To decide from which namespace a `<cadence>` tag comes and to write it back out into the same namespace, the `GPXfield` class describing it would have to become namespace-aware. So would the parser to use the correct `GPXExtension` for a given element depending on its namespace. This would be required to  _allow to losslesly read and write back out any well-formed gpx_.

**For reading and writing an extension in the spirit of the present API, the schema of any GPX to be read needs to be known beforehand.**
I see no way to losslessly take in the data of yet un-implemented extensions just using the present library design. Without being able to read and write back out unknown extensions, the parser does not  _allow to losslesly read and write back out any well-formed gpx_. Since extensions can use all XML features I agree with @nawagers that using ElementTree (or another library covering XML, ideally from the standard library) would be the way to go. ElementTree has been in the standard library since 2.5 so using it would not pull in external requirements. Storing the whole extension data would therefore be much easier if one could end minidom support (otherwise one would have to convert minidom nodes to ElementTree nodes or vice-versa). 
@tkrajina: are there reasons that @nawagers and I overlooked for keeping minidom, or is it just there for historical reasons? If ElementTree passes the unit tests as a drop-in for lxml for python 2.6 onwards, would minidom still be required?

**Convenience layer for extensions**
I think the most transparent way to implement things is to keep all extension classes as close as possible to the xml schema of the extension itsself and allow direct access to the developer. Therefore I agree with @nawagers, that maybe the most elegant way is to store the whole subtree below the `extensions` in an ElementTree. The convenience functions for reading could then be reduced to a list of common searches in the ElementTree to find the most common types of information, maybe available as @properties of the GPXExtensions object. Possibly the searches could be annotated to return sensible data types (floats for floats, ints for ints, unicode/strings for strings). Writing could stay restricted to direct access to the ElementTree, as for writing developers need to chose between namespaces to write in, the will be forced to find out about their structure anyway. Possibly overwriting existing attributes could still be implemented as a convenience function (by searching and overwriting the content of the existing element in the existing namespace).
If there are design considerations about gpxpy API to take into account (to make working with extensions more alike to tracksegments or trackpoints), one could do that by a wrapper around the ElementTree.

Thanks for the help with is_subtype!
The following method passes all core tests:

```python
def get_all_subclasses(typ, recursive: bool = True, memo = None) -> List[Type[Any]]:
    memo = memo or set()

    # if we have collected the subclasses for this already, return
    if typ in memo:
        return []

    # else remember that we have collected them, and collect them
    memo.add(typ)
    if is_generic_type(typ):
        # We now use get_origin() to also find all the concrete subclasses in case the desired type is a generic
        sub_list = get_origin(typ).__subclasses__()
    else:
        sub_list = typ.__subclasses__()

    # recurse
    result = [t for t in sub_list if t is not typ and is_subtype(t, typ, bound_typevars={})]
    if recursive:
        for typpp in sub_list:
            for t in get_all_subclasses(typpp, recursive=True, memo=memo):
                # unfortunately we have to check 't not in sub_list' because with generics strange things happen
                # also is_subtype returns false when the parent is a generic
                if t not in sub_list and is_subtype(t, typ, bound_typevars={}):
                    result.append(t)

    return result
```

Concerning your last comment, that's what I call the 'intermediate classes'. The only reason why `FullUnparam[~T, int]`, `FullUnparam[str, int]` and `HalfParam[str]` appear in the list is that these types were used to construct our classes. But many other variants virtually exist (with all possible replacements of the typevars!), and they do not appear because they were not used. Thanks for spotting this issue. You're more than welcome to help out, my current priority is on fixing Python 3.7 support.

Some things to keep in mind:

* For Python 2.7, we convert stubfiles to a 2.7 parsable form, i.e. using type comments and ``pass`` instead of ``...``. These are potentially stored with .pyi2 suffix to avoid conflicts with Python 3 stubfiles in the same folder. See https://github.com/Stewori/pytypes#python-27-compliant-stubfiles.
* In some situations (something with running module initialization code in a module involving forward references in stubfiles) certain checks must be delayed until module import is finished. In that case, pytypes monkeypatches ``builtins.__import__`` for detecting end of module import. (This shall be turned into an import hook, but that's still a todo.) Just pointing this out, hope it does not interfere with your approach. The monkeypatch is only applied if such a situation really comes up. Also see ``pytypes.import_hook_enabled`` for controlling this behavior.
* Currently pytypes also works with Jython. Keeping this compatibility valid can be tricky. I hope we can find a solution without breaking this support.
* ``pytypes.stubfile_manager`` applies a bit more magic than just loading the stubfile. When classes are used as types or type parameters it assures that not the class objects from the stubfile are used but instead the corresponding class objects from the actual module. It was tricky to get this right for nested classes, which lead to somewhat complicated code in stubfile_manager.py. It might take additional consideration to integrate your approach with this mechanism. See e.g. ``stubfile_manager._match_stub_type`` or ``stubfile_manager._match_classes``.

I cannot tell right now if any of these notes applies to your approach (e.g. I never tried to use contextlib in Jython, maybe it just works). It's just things that *might* have to be considered. Good luck and feel free to ask for advice on pytypes internals etc if required!

> You actually totally can (since they're both based on OS Threads), but only QThreads can receive signals. 

Both threading.Thread and QThread are indeed wrappers around OS threads. But infrastructures they set up are different: threading.Thread cannot have Qt event loop.

> In practice then it's highly impractical to not use QThreads when you're using PyQt.

I did `sudo pip3 install telepot`, and saw:

```
Collecting telepot
  Downloading https://www.piwheels.org/simple/telepot/telepot-12.6-py3-none-any.whl (41kB)
    100% |████████████████████████████████| 51kB 156kB/s
Requirement already satisfied: urllib3>=1.9.1 in /usr/lib/python3/dist-packages (from telepot)
Installing collected packages: telepot
Successfully installed telepot-12.6
```

It's downloading from piwheels.org, a repository hosting *pre-compiled* python packages. Generally, it's a good idea to download from there, to save Pi the trouble of compiling native code. For libraries containing a lot of native code, compiling on Pi can take a long time. Downloading the pre-compiled version from piwheels.org can save a lot of time.

However, in the case of telepot, the version on piwheels.org somehow is missing the async version (that is, module `telepot.aio`). That's why you got the error.

Here is how to tell `pip3` *not* to use piwheels.org. `sudo nano /etc/pip.conf` should give:
```
[global]
extra-index-url=https://www.piwheels.org/simple
```
**Comment out both lines** by placing a `#` in front of both lines. Save and exit the editor.

Then, remove the existing telepot:
```
sudo pip3 uninstall telepot
```

Install again:
```
sudo pip3 install telepot
```

There should be much more output this time. Below are only the first few lines:
```
Collecting telepot
  Downloading telepot-12.6.tar.gz (73kB)
    100% |████████████████████████████████| 81kB 594kB/s
Collecting aiohttp>=2.0.0 (from telepot)
  Downloading aiohttp-3.1.3.tar.gz (756kB)
    100% |████████████████████████████████| 757kB 159kB/s
```

This one should include the async version (`telepot.aio`). Running the examples should be fine.

Afterward, you may want to edit `/etc/pip.conf` back to using piwheels.org. It's up to you. I think it's a good idea in general.

Discord doesn't tend to like people disconnecting and reconnecting often, so if you're doing this I'd suggest spacing out how often you connect quite a bit (connecting only once every 10 minutes would clear you of any chance of getting booted off the websocket for it).

As for the actual script side, you can connect to Discord using `client.run` as before but make an `on_ready` definition (or a background task that uses `wait_for_ready`) that grabs your information and then uses `await client.logout()` to sign out.
Note that logging out **closes the event loop** so you'll need to create a new one if you want to reconnect (you can do this easily by just putting the following before you `.run()`:)
```py
asyncio.set_event_loop(asyncio.new_event_loop())
```

I'd like to reopen this pull request (or something like it) because Android uses OS-specific .dynamic tags 0x6000000f and 0x60000010 as `DT_ANDROID_REL` and `DT_ANDROID_RELSZ` respectively. This conflicts with the `DT_SUNW_FILTER` usage as a string table index. It is unsafe to assume that an OS-specific .dynamic entry must be a string table index when not all OSes comply with this usage.

Ok, joining this thread a bit late but here is my 2 cents:

It seems to me that with respect to this question we are discussing two separate things:
1. Issue #20 - (Debian packages)
2. Potential licensing problem of including pyasn alongside other software.

The latest version of Debian already includes the problematic code and (going by the advice of the FOSS license expert that @hadiasghari talked to) the code it is not a main feature of Debian, therefore no problem on the advertising clause. Pyasn itself has no problem either and correctly includes the acknowledgements. Even if it were packaged along side Debian it would not be a main feature. So following the same logic, with respect to issue #20, I think that we should be able to move on as far as my understanding goes. Of course I'm in no way a licensing expert. @mdbloice Many thanks for your fast response. I revised the sample function to save the processed images using the index of processing sequence. For the index, I first count the number of digits contained in "n" and fill some ''0"s in the left side of the index. Is it your convenience to provide a function like this? The "sample" function save the processed image inside "pipeline._execute" using a random name. In this case, it is difficult to match the images sampled in two different batches using the same seed. It would be helpful if we could using the same naming rule for the two batches.

# Count the number of digits contained in a given number
def num_digits(n):
    import math
    num = int(math.log10(n)) + 1

    return num

# Sample "n" augmentated images using the pipeline. Images will be saved using increasing indices
def sample(pipeline, n):
    """
    Generate :attr:`n` number of samples from the current pipeline.

    This function samples from the pipeline, using the original images
    defined during instantiation. All images generated by the pipeline
    are by default stored in an ``output`` directory, relative to the
    path defined during the pipeline's instantiation.

    :param n: The number of new samples to produce.
    :type n: Integer
    :return: None
    """
    if len(pipeline.augmentor_images) == 0:
        raise IndexError("There are no images in the pipeline. "
                         "Add a directory using add_directory(), "
                         "pointing it to a directory containing images.")

    if len(pipeline.operations) == 0:
        raise IndexError("There are no operations associated with this pipeline.")

    # Use the number of digits to fill "0"s in the left of the index of each sampled image
    n_digits = num_digits(n)
    
    sample_count = 1
    progress_bar = tqdm(total=n, desc="Executing Pipeline", unit=' Samples', leave=False)
    while sample_count <= n:
        for augmentor_image in pipeline.augmentor_images:
            if sample_count <= n:
                # Do not save the processed image inside the function
                image = pipeline._execute(augmentor_image, save_to_disk = False)                

                ## Save the processed image below
                # Combine the index with extension
                file_name = str(sample_count)
                file_name = file_name.zfill(n_digits);
                file_name = file_name + "." + pipeline.save_format
                try:
                    # A strange error is forcing me to do this at the moment, but will fix later properly
                    # TODO: Fix this!
                    if image.mode != "RGB":
                        image = image.convert("RGB")

                    # Remove directory
                    original_file_name = os.path.basename(augmentor_image.image_path)
                    # Split the file name to file name and extension
                    original_file_name = os.path.splitext(original_file_name)
                    # Only retain the file name without extension
                    original_file_name = original_file_name[0]
                    # Combine the file name with the combination of the index and extension
                    file_name = original_file_name + "_" + file_name
                    # Combine with the directory
                    file_name = os.path.join(augmentor_image.output_directory, file_name)                    
                    image.save(file_name, pipeline.save_format)
                except IOError:
                    print("Error writing %s." % file_name)
                
                file_name_to_print = os.path.basename(augmentor_image.image_path)
                # This is just to shorten very long file names which obscure the progress bar.
                if len(file_name_to_print) >= 30:
                    file_name_to_print = file_name_to_print[0:10] + "..." + \
                                         file_name_to_print[-10: len(file_name_to_print)]
                progress_bar.set_description("Processing %s" % file_name_to_print)
                progress_bar.update(1)
            sample_count += 1
    progress_bar.close()

I also noticed that pyodbc also produces strange results for a query of the form

```
SELECT column FROM table
ORDER BY other_column
```

When fetching results, `other_column` is also includes in the row-tuples. This is not the case when using another ODBC or JDBC client.

I think unicode_results is understandable: it instructs pyODBC to return 
everything as unicode results which is an understandable, logical and 
expectable process, especially in python2.x.

For python3.x it's an understandable flag because, although it should 
default to true, setting it to false clearly indicates that the library 
will not return unicode anymore. It's about as descriptive and 
appropriate as it gets. My personal opinion is tweaking anything more in 
names would complicate stuff, you'd get discussions about the 
unicode_results flag as opposed to the char_as_bytes flags and people 
would have whole philosophies about what's the difference.

As for your GUID and XML types: I expect there would never be an 
incompatabilty issue with GUID's because they are all convertable to 
ascii- and back no matter how you present them, so either unicode or 
bytes would be 100% usable.

I'm not sure about the XML types so I'm no expert but it seems that at 
least SQL Server has different possible encodings, if you pass in UTF-8 
you receive UTF-8 and if you pass in ANSI you receive ANSI:

https://msdn.microsoft.com/en-us/library/ms131375.aspx

This means you'd have to make an assumption about the encoding.

If I would have to decide this I would both send also GUIDs and XML as 
bytes, XML because it just might be what people need in a certain 
scenario and GUIDs just because of consistency. It would make the flag 
more understandable: you set 'unicode_results' to False and you know 
what to expect without an exception here and an exception there.

Robert

However, there is [an issue](https://github.com/thisbejim/Pyrebase/issues/115) where reauthenticating and restarting a stream causes all that data to hit the stream handler a second time. We can't stop Firebase from sending that information but we can filter it out, follow along with the progress of that issue to see when this is fixed.

Thanks for pointing that out @pepoluan, you are absolutely right, NOTRACK is indeed an alias for the CT target. 

I would like to point out that to perform that "NOTRACK" action, one could insert the rule in 2 different ways, however only the former seems to be properly processed by iptc.
```
# iptables -t raw -A PREROUTING -p 132 -j CT --notrack
# iptables -t raw -A PREROUTING -p 132 -j NOTRACK
```

The supported way then to handle the NOTRACK is as follows:
```
import iptc

rule = iptc.Rule()
rule.protocol = "132"
rule.target = iptc.Target(rule, "CT")
rule.target.notrack = ""
chain = iptc.Chain(iptc.Table(iptc.Table.RAW), "PREROUTING")
chain.insert_rule(rule)
```

Shall we then close this issue or is there any way of adding support for the alias processing?

The first example is correctly formatted form data (see: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/POST_(HTTP)#Use_for_submitting_web_forms).  The second example is JSON content that expresses the same *data* but is not form encoded, so it is not valid.

Here is the Python source for `pipes.quote()`: 

```python
# Reliably quote a string as a single argument for /bin/sh

# Safe unquoted
_safechars = frozenset(string.ascii_letters + string.digits + '@%_-+=:,./')


def quote(file):
    """Return a shell-escaped version of the file string."""
    for c in file:
        if c not in _safechars:
            break
    else:
        if not file:
            return "''"
        return file

    # use single quotes, and put single quotes into double quotes
    # the string $'b is then quoted as '$'"'"'b'
    return "'" + file.replace("'", "'\"'\"'") + "'"
```

資料夾的建立日期不會是相同的，除非你開兩個程式同時抓同一份漫畫。

章節的排列順序也**不是**按照網頁的建立順序排序，而是按漫畫網站上給出的順序。若網站把番外排在一起，扒下來時番外也會排在一起。

承上，網站本身也不一定按推出時間排序，缺集數、未漢化、一次更新多集、先出連載再出單行本等等情況都有。

Comic Crawler 沒有能力得知每章推出的時間，它只知道網頁長什麼樣子，和之前的紀錄相比是否有章節被增加/移除而已。承上，若網站本身不按推出時間排序，以流水號編號實在沒多大意義。

---

按照資料夾的建立日期排列也就相當於按照章節列表排列，因為章節資料夾只有在下載圖片時才會被建立。既然下載時是按照章節列表下載，資料夾的建立時間也就按章節列表排序。

Do I get You right: What You basically want is some kind of template
mechanism to customize the output of directives? For instance to create Your
own class, namespace, group, and whatever index pages instead of following a
fixed structure?

Some thoughts about Your above example: There is documentation for people
how "use" the code and there is documentation for people who are supposed to
work on it; a big difference in my opinion. Users would likely not digg into
the code, but prefer a clean and neat html documentation. In that case, it
is not just some kind of by-product, but an indispensable part needing the
same level of attention and effort as the source code itself.

It is one way to put sophisticated documentation as near as possible to the
source code, e.g. description beside struct members (does literate
programming ring a bell?), but it often makes the code harder to read, too
and bloats header files.
So, if the table from above is meant for "users", then just see it as an
integral document and write it separately in rst or doxygen table markup. If
it is ment for developers who make changes to the code, don't waste Your
time with an elaborate html documentation. They are experts and required to
know the user documentation anyway. Keep the code readable and sprinkle
(carefully) some non-doxygen comments into it where it is not self-explaining.

@djmattyg007 you cannot compare youtube-dl with wormhole and claim both work like the same. Let me explain this below.

First of all, youtube-dl has *no* `install_requires` declared in its `setup.py`. This means that it can run off a basic Python installation, and doesn't use anything but the standard library. wormhole on the other hand does have a [bunch of requirements](https://github.com/warner/magic-wormhole/blob/master/setup.py#L28-L40). It won't run with just a basic standard library, it requires several modules of which some depend on compiled binaries. Some dependencies have themselves dependencies which require other libraries that need to be installed on the system or bundled with wormhole.

Thus, the following claims are just wrong:

>  * Works out of the box, only need python installed (which almost all Linux distributions have pre-installed)
>  * No unpacking or installation necessary
>  * No system libraries changed
>  * The python executable script is usually small enough that delta updates are unnecessary
>  * Works on Live ISOs (python is more likely to be installed than anything to manage AppImages)
>  * Can use the same python executable script file when dual-booting multiple distributions

As you can see, there's more to do than just creating a Python egg and ship that. There's tons of dependencies that, for a portable application bundle, need to be distributed, too. Thus, the UX of a Python egg in comparison to an application bundle is way worse.

Design-wise, I'd prefer to have a few number of methods in the `Topology` class exposed to the user. The behavior of these methods change depending on how the `Topology` is initialized (`static=True`, etc.)

By maintaining a strict set of methods exposed in the Topology class, it's easier to swap-out topologies whenever needed in the `GeneralOptimizer`.

I understand your point that we should not compute the neighbor information for each iteration, and there should be a way to store it. And this requires passing the `Swarm` information inside the `Topology` class. I maintain that the two classes should remain separated.

To add to your suggestion, what if we add an optional `swarm` parameter (default is `None`) during 
`__init__()` and raise an error whenever `static==True` and `swarm==None`? 

```python
# ring.py
def __init__(static=False, swarm=None):
    if static and swarm==None:
        raise ValueError
```

In that case, we can create a `neighbor_idx` inside the `Topology` class whenever `static==True`. We then use that whenever we compute for the best scores etc. What do you think about this?

@Alireza2n 

> But again we're changing the original signature of method, comparing to original strftime(). I dont know if changing signature is a bad practice or not.

It's not necessarily bad practice, however (quoting the project description):

> jdatetime is Jalali implementation of Python's datetime module

and
 
> This module exactly follows Python Standard datetime module's methods 

So it's preferred to avoid any deviation from the standard library API as much as possible. IMO adding extra class/module level functions is fine, even extending the `__init__` seems less than a deviation than changing signature of random methods.
In this case, since the desired behavior can be achieved outside of the library, it would not justify to change the signature of `strftime`.

An example of such solution is by adding the `locale` property to the `jdatetime` class, and providing an extra method on `jdatetime` class, say `aslocale()` (following [astimezone](https://docs.python.org/release/2.7.1/library/datetime.html#datetime.datetime.astimezone)) to return a `jdatetime` instance with the same timestamp, but in a different `locale`. Then the user of the library (say a Django template filter, etc.) decides the locale (say from request, DB, etc.) and calls `aslocale`, before `strftime`:

```python
value.aslocale(locale).strftime(fmt)
```

This way we even won't need to change the signature of `__init__`. 

On the other hand this seems a valid and useful feature to have for `jdatetime` to select the default `locale` in a way other than only the environment (system or process level locale), so we can work on that.

Note that the CESU-8/Java-UTF-8 decoder in ftfy.bad_codecs does not enforce correctness, and is documented as being explicitly intended not to do so.

Here's an example of a byte sequence that is invalid CESU-8 and is rejected by Java, but is accepted by ftfy's decoder:
```
import ftfy.bad_codecs
print(b'\xf0\x90\x80\x80'.decode("java_utf8", errors="strict") == u"\U00010000") # True
```
So be careful not to rely on the codec to make accept/reject decisions about the validity of serialized objects ...

with reversion.create_revision():

            reversion.add_meta(ProxyUserRevisionMeta, simple_user=user)
            reversion.set_user(None)
            reversion.set_comment('my comment')

            # do instance saving...

Hi @sergei-dyshel,

From what I know, it's not possible to override a user directly passed to the `ssh` command from the command line; the priority is `command line > host-specific configuration > global configuration`.

It is possible to allow username aliasing when using the `assh wrapper`, so it will only work when using a terminal and having the proper configuration.
It won't work with other binaries using the libssh itself (`scp`, `rsync`, and everything with a graphical interface.

I like the idea. However, I think it will introduce some inconsistency in the usage of ssh.

The issue is most likely that dlib was compiled with support for AVX and/or SSE4 instructions, but your cpu is too old to support them (pre-2011, I think).

The easiest solution is to download dlib and compile it yourself. But when you do that, you need to make one change.

Before compiling dlib, edit dlib's tools/python/CMakeLists.txt file from:

```
set(USE_SSE4_INSTRUCTIONS ON CACHE BOOL "Use SSE4 instructions")
```

to:

```
set(USE_SSE2_INSTRUCTIONS ON CACHE BOOL "Use SSE2 instructions")
```

And then compile and install dlib and the dlib python extensions. Hopefully that should fix it.

In my experience there is no consistent naming scheme and no easy way to reliably automatically pick the best stream from those discovered in a nested playlist. Ideally the stations would just list their streams from top to bottom in order of quality but they clearly don't all do this as your example demonstrates. Maybe they've found that 256k wasn't supported by some clients, or maybe not worth the extra bandwidth for some content types - who knows.

I've thought previously about modelling things slightly differently and thinking of a station as an album or an artist. An artist might make the most sense as each station (artist) would have a number of shows (albums) which are composed of a number of streams (tracks). I think it works for browsing but falls apart for searching. It'd also be quite different from other backends and, depending on the client, might be a very confusing user experience. You could easily end up with many tracks for each station cluttering your search results and making it much harder to find what you want. Some webclients may ignore the album and artist fields of search results; I can't remember what mpd clients handle here.  Perhaps it's a bad idea.

Modelling as a playlist doesn't really work for the same reasons, plus you cannot return playlists from search results.

Thanks a lot neal for your input benchmark! I had a quick look, it
appears the masked expressions are not very efficient in pythran (but
still better than numpy, as shown by your bench).

I rewrote your numpy expression as the following:

def limit (x, epsilon=1e-6):
    # note the use of where here
    return np.where( np.abs(x) < epsilon, 0, x/np.abs(x))

#pythran export clip0(complex128[], float64)
def clip0 (z, _max):
    mask = np.abs(z) > _max
## print ('#clipped:', np.sum(mask))
    z[mask] = (limit (z[mask]) * _max)
    return z

Under numpy, it's not as clever as it could, as x/np.abs(x) is computed
for all x values. Under Pythran however, we have the opportunity to
lazyly evaluate the expression so that only the relevant part are
computed. this is not done because of a not so good implementation of
numpy.where, but we have an opportunity here.

About why it works, I suspect `user_data` to never be used as if it is to contain python objects it would require complex casting. Maybe you are using it ?

Otherwise, it comes from the actual callbacks, and here I wonder if there would not be another issue in the code using python-libusb1 here: interpreter shutdown is extremely hard to get right (especially, `__del__` may be called in any order at any time regardless of what still holds references to what), which is why several python-libusb1 classes are context managers. You may want to check that you are using these as such, or you may indeed encounter interpreter shutdown crashes. For example, the callback python callables could get garbage-collected before libusb is deinitialised. This would not explain why proposed patch would improve the situation, though.

In any case this change, modulo the `c_void_p()` simplification, looks good independently from any other issue.

To me, it makes more sense to put the logic of the expiration extension in the `authenticate_credentials` method.
```
class KnoxTokenAuthentication(TokenAuthentication):
    def authenticate_credentials(self, token):
        result = super().authenticate_credentials(token)
        if result:
            user, auth_token = result
            current_expiry = auth_token.expires
            new_expiry = timezone.now() + knox_settings.TOKEN_TTL
            auth_token.expires = new_expiry
            # Update once per second max
            if (new_expiry - current_expiry).total_seconds() > 1:
                auth_token.save(update_fields=('expires',))
        return result
```

Hi!

Thanks for your input. My memory on this project is a little cloudy. IIRC the primary need for me to work on this library was the requirement to ensure that the application logs certain events and thus the log lines had to be caught. Now you're proposing a second use case for this library to catch any log lines and output logs for failing tests. 

I like the idea since it seems to me to be very useful and thus would love to see a patch for it and also would merge it if you would be so kind to provide one. I would suggest a different approach to it though to keep the explanation of the feature simple. I'll try to collect some thoughts on this here:

* I think it is always a desired behavior to receive all logged events for a failed test IMHO. Following that thought means there is no need to configure a minimum level for failed tests. 
* It would also be nice to still be able to silence the log output altogether so you won't litter the logs on your CI system.
* It would still be nice to see logs of a certain level on your local development system to spot possible errors. 
* To enable the behavior on a global scope that any log output may lead to a non successful state of the test seems not that good to me TBH. It may even be the case that an error event here and a warning event there is totally fine because you're testing a fallback behavior for a failed condition. If this scenario stays valid it means that a global configuration for this may not fit the need of all your tests. The need to fail tests if an error/warning/critical event was caught can be realized through an assert though.

It seems to me to be quite the difficult endeavor and there is further need to iron out the remaining wrinkles in the proposal. I hope the text was understandable and constructive to you and didn't kill your motivation regarding this issue. It was a tiresome workday for me so I might have overlooked something. In general the code looks fine. I don't have really an objection regarding using `generic_visit`. The main issue I have is that it never complains now about a missing import if it is not necessary. I for example wouldn't want that because I want to enforce a specific set of imports on all files even if they are not necessary there, but just to be consistent.

Regarding the tests, I looked at your Travis 2.7 build and I use the line numbers of the repo at that time. Your patch is adding one line so your lines will be at the moment one line lower. The lines 50-52 check that the imports reported don't mention any unknown future imports (51 and 52) and line 50 checks that it only mentions each import at most once (e.g. don't mention the same import as missing and present).

Then in line 61 and 62 (where you got errors in 2.7) it checks that it returns a complementary set of errors. There is a set *A* of available imports in Python (`all_available`) and then checks that the combination of the sets *P* (errors that an import is present) and *M* (errors that an import is missing) is the same set *A*. Basically that there is an error message for each import possible (via line 50 we already checked that *P* and *M* have no intersection).

Now your code is not always reporting all missing imports and so it'll fail in line 61 where it checks that the set *M* is equal to *A - U* (*U* are the imports used). And at the moment there is no test which is verifying that it can ignore codes (in which case *M* would be *A - U - Im* where *Im* is the set of ignored missing imports.

Now as I mention in the first paragraph I'd prefer if there is an option to modify this automatic and in that case the current tests could disable your changes and it should work. Alternatively you need to modify `run_test` or `check_result` in such a way that it expects that certain “missing errors” are missing because the code doesn't require them. You could also modify `generate_code` to actually require the imports (e.g. by doing division).

By the way the lines 142 and 142 works similar to the lines 61 and 62. By default it always expects all imports to be missing (expect for the test number 10 in which case it doesn't expect `absolute_import` and `print_function`) defined via `expected_imports` in lines 133 and 134.

I hope this helps you to identify what to change your PR to make it compatible :)

I don't need this feature anymore but other might. In fact I didn't end up using django-websocket-redis at all.

Once I saw how easy it was to use websockets with uWSGI I moved over my runserver command to uWSGI as well (I don't use the built in runserver for dev due to previously mentioned SSL + inotify issues).

[This recent patch to uWSGI](https://github.com/unbit/uwsgi/pull/986) makes it super easy use uWSGI websockets with gevent for a completely non blocking main loop.

To handle auth I use an XMLHttpRequest before opening the websocket to obtain a short lived token from a Django view that is then stored in redis and given to the user (I got this idea from playing with services like PubNub). Thus my main loop never hits in the database - in fact it's only imports are gevent, uwsgi, redis, and the stdlib.

Also inspired by [this SO question](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/7196306/competing-consumer-on-redis-pub-sub-supported), instead of just using publish and listen I use rpush/publish and listen/lpop so that I can have multiple consumers without collisions. Thus redis handles all the atomic locking stuff so again it's all super simple.

> My point is there could be quite normal use-cases when developer desires to send a message to socket.io client from a separate (not eventloop) thread.

I don't see this request as Socket.IO specific. Basically you are asking to support communication across system threads for eventlet/gevent, which might be already possible, I haven't really looked into it. Though its application is going to be severely limited, since you can only run code that doesn't lock the GIL on that other thread.

> Just to make my point clear I mentioned Tornado server implementation

I think a solution for your problem should not be implement here, I see it as a request for eventlet or gevent. But note that async programming with callbacks like your Tornado example is a completely different paradigm than what these frameworks propose. I suppose with greenlets this could work as follows:

    def my_greenlet():
        result = start_system_thread(my_thread_function, my_args)
        emit('result', data=result)

So this function would go to sleep until the other thread ends. As I said above, it may be possible to implement this, it depends on having a way for another thread to send a signal to the thread running the greenlets, while the signal itself is greenlet aware. And a big unknown on how this might affect performance due to the GIL.

I understand the motivation for moving tests to nightly. The concern I still have is if we disable certain tests on PR-level and only run them nightly, the problems in code may not surface in PR as they should. One might submit a patch for optimization or fix on existing op which actually contains bug, whose tests only run nightly. Reviewers might assume that the tests are run by the CI while in fact they are not. In general, removing tests from CI in PR is equivalent of removing the guarantee that the particular functionality still works after the PR.

Admittedly some tests, including some of the tests I wrote, may be simple fuzzy tests with randomized inputs, that should have been designed as small fixtures to catch specific edge cases. That way, the each of the tests won't run for longer than seconds which is ideal. I agree we should do better at writing tests. Given the current situation in the test cases, it will be major endeavor moving from where we are to where the ideal world is. Given the constraint that we shouldn't be breaking mxnet in doing so, I think we should only replace existing long-running tests with carefully designed test cases instead of disabling them.

@piiswrong I'm adding a check to the SimpleBind call in the backend. The forward-pass-only workloads such as with TensorRT work fine if `grad_req` is set to `null` in Python. If grad_req is not set or is set to `write` or `add`, `SimpleBind` fails without any useful message. So, now I do the following:

1) If `MXNET_USE_TENSORRT` is set, a check is made that all elements of `grad_req_type_vec` have a value of kNullOp. If they don't then an exception is thrown with a helpful message, which suggests 2 courses of action:
  - if this is an inference-only graph, the user is told to set `grad_req` to `null`
  - if the graph is a training graph, then the user is told to unset MXNET_USE_TENSORRT

Here is the message that will be printed:

[17:50:46] src/executor/graph_executor.cc:1163: MXNET_USE_TENSORRT set but graph is not inference-only. If it is an inference graph, set grad_req to null during simple_bind call. If it is a training graph, unset the MXNET_USE_TENSORRT env variable

2) If `MXNET_USE_TENSORRT` is set and `grad_req` is `null`, then calling `backward` on the symbol will have no effect. Also, if `grad_req` is `null`, the `forward()` call will work whether `is_train` is `True` or `False`. This ensures that the user isn't burdened with configuration, as long as the binding is explicitly set for an inference-only graph (`grad_req = null`).

I hope this is what you're looking for in terms of guarding that the user only run an inference graph, and that there is a helpful message as to what to do if the user tries running a training graph.

The code doing the check is [here](https://github.com/mkolod/incubator-mxnet/commit/3b1e15f6e1a68c26b25be76ee69ce52aba579860).

Hi all,

By default it does not say that no additional fields should be present. --> it does not even say that additional fields should be present

This is by design and allows for forward compatibility: you can add new fields to a response and clients using an older version of the spec will not complain about the response containing those fields. --> I do not think it is the right way to allow forward compatibility (especially in my case that I am developing a server). And I am not saying to remove it, but to allow people to choose how to do the validation.

It sounds like you found a solution for your use case. I don't think it makes sense for us to allow you to override something you set in the spec by a config value, in essence not following the spec anymore. Is it really so hard to set it to false? --> I have to recursive iterate on all the sub-documents aswell, and it is not really clean

I think that adding this feature could only improve the product (i.e. Cerberus json validator provides it with the ALLOW_UNKNOWN parameter). I am not using Cerberus, because Bravado is more flexible and allow me to create strong validation rule with less code.

Regards,
Amedeo

This is a tricky one. First of all requirements-dev.txt only contains additional packages needed for developing bravado (like packages required for running the tests). cryptography is not one of them. This highlights a problem of Python packaging: we can't specify dependencies based on Linux distribution version. The same issue arises if you try to use bravado on Python 2.6: it will fetch Twisted which will error out on installation since it doesn't support Python 2.6 anymore.

I think this is something you need to fix locally. What we do internally is run our own PyPi server that only contains the package versions we want to use. Otherwise it'd be way too restrictive for users that do put in the work to stay up to date with their software stack. We don't want them to be stuck on an old cryptography version (or on an old Twisted version).

(benchmark) C02QJ1JPG8WL:bravado-core maci$ python -m timeit -v -n 1000000 -s 'from tests.profiling.conftest import large_pets; from collections import Mapping; d = large_pets(200)' 'isinstance(d, Mapping)'

raw times: 1.6 1.62 1.62
1000000 loops, best of 3: 1.6 usec per loop
(benchmark) C02QJ1JPG8WL:bravado-core maci$ python -m timeit -v -n 1000000 -s 'from tests.profiling.conftest import large_pets; from collections import Mapping; d = large_pets(200)' 'isinstance(d, dict) or isinstance(d, Mapping)'
raw times: 1.68 1.66 1.66
1000000 loops, best of 3: 1.66 usec per loop
(benchmark) C02QJ1JPG8WL:bravado-core maci$ python -m timeit -v -n 1000000 -s 'from tests.profiling.conftest import large_pets; from collections import Mapping; d = large_pets(200)' 'isinstance(d, dict)'
raw times: 0.158 0.17 0.168
1000000 loops, best of 3: 0.158 usec per loop
(benchmark) C02QJ1JPG8WL:bravado-core maci$
```
According to the above results ``isinstance(d, (dict, Mapping))`` has no improvements respect ``isinstance(d, Mapping)``, instead ``isinstance(d, dict)`` is about 10 times faster.
So in order to improve performances in case of instance is dict (majority of the cases) we should use a code similar to
```python
def is_dict_like(spec):
    if isinstance(spec, dict):
        return True
    return isinstance(spec, Mapping)
```

Hi @Legomaniac, sorry for the late reply, it seems that I skipped the notification for this issue.
I've been thinking about this exact feature for a while now. I think it would be very valuable to have. Implementation is somewhat tricky, we basically have 3 options:

1. create a top level `unencrypted` key, under which every is in clear

2. allow prepending a suffix such as `_clear` to keys in the tree, which would force sops to skip that branch entirely (everything underneat it would be in cleartext)

3. support some form of pattern matching that's user configurable to define what should be in clear and/or encrypted. For example, starting `sops` with `--unencrypted "key1|somekey2"` would treat the flag value as a regex to leave both "key1" and "somekey2" in cleartext. Similarly, we could have a flag `--default-unencrypted` paired with `--encrypt "key1|somekey2"` to leave the whole document in clear except for "key1" and "somekey2". This option is a lot more complex to implement that 1 or 2, but provides more flexibility.

I have a small preference for option 2, but could be convinced that other options are better.

@lissyx I mostly use Xcode as my IDE and rarely have to touch any text-based part of the build infrastructure it uses. 

As of my last test, the binary distribution for Deep Speech had all the dependencies apart from lib sox shipping with it, ut they had paths to those dependencies would only work on the original build machine. 

And no, moving dynamic libraries around to get a working build product is not the usual way. At least you don’t do it manually. Xcode usually does this for you to bundle everything together for packaging or distribution. Just have a look an app on macOS. It’s actually a directory called a “package” you can open via the contextual menu in the Finder. Having all non-system-provided dependencies in there bundled up makes things relatively neat. 

I believe you were right before when you suggested using `@rpath`, but I didn’t want to pile on even more dynamic linker magic (I seems just to result in the search of a list of paths) that might break. For our scenario here, `@executable_path` will work fine.

Fixed by #233.

> We allow using any function as listener when using the observe method instead of the decorator.

We do the same thing in Atom. The `observe` function checks if the listener is a plain function. If it is, it's just added to the listener list. Otherwise, a method is broken apart and wrapped in an object as described above. The object is callable, so the dispatching code can treat it the same as function. 

Additionally, it reimplements `__eq__` to return  `False` when the weakref'd instance is collected so the manager knows the wrapper can be removed. It looks very similar to the class @tacaswell posted. Atom's is contained in the `methodwrapper.cpp` listed above.

I don't think the implementation is correct in this way.
1) In the example, magnitudes above the maximum (>8) are filtered above 200km. This is not how it should be, as these magnitudes should not be filtered at all. The filter should be applied only below the prescribed magnitude value (in this case only below 8)
2) The bonus is also not correct in my opinion. Discarding all magnitudes below a threshold is not necessary, as this is controlled by the minimum magnitude parameters of the source. Moreover, as in the previous case, the 200km filter should be applied below 4, and not above, as indicated.
I suspect you're filtering in the wrong direction. To summarise: magnitudes BELOW a give threshold value cannot generate sensible ground motion ABOVE a certain distance, and should be filtered out, not vice-versa.

@jaypatel512 ended up having to give up on the rest framework and go classic because of backwards-compatibility with existing subscriptions. But. Basically you need to implement the following items:

* [`__nonzero__`](https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html#object.__nonzero__) so that a resource can be empty or not based on its internal state. ([`__bool__`](https://docs.python.org/3/reference/datamodel.html#object.__bool__) in python 3.)
* [`__iter__`](https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html#object.__iter__) so that resources that are iterable can be actually iterated over instead of falling back on `__getitem__`.
* [`__getitem__`](https://docs.python.org/2/reference/datamodel.html#object.__getitem__) implementation altered so that it behaves appropriately depending on whether the resource is, internally, a mapping object or an indexed object. (This could obviate the need for implementing `__iter__`)

The use case for threads is the Dropbox Client. We have a debug version that lets a developer start and stop type collection using a menu entry. This is handy so you can collect types for a specific sequence of operations in the app (but not for the rather long app startup sequence). The init() method needs to be called when the app initializes itself, because the app creates long-running threads. If init() was implicitly called by start(), we would collect no types from threads that have already started.

As a compromise, I think it's fine to have start() call init() if it hasn't been called yet -- we must make sure that calling init() multiple times is okay.

Note that init() also has a optional argument used to filter filenames. This makes more sense here than for start(). Similarly, dump_stats() is separate because it takes an output filename. (You can use dumps_stats() if you want to do something else with the data -- though I think it's a shame there's no API to get the JSON as a dict instead of as a string.)

What we could also use is an API that resets all the global state -- similar to what collecting_types() in pyannotate_runtime/tests/test_collect_types.py does.

So, all in all I am open to API changes but I want certain functionality to be available.

>
> Could you share the code? I don't quite understand, how finding the label
> helps one to click the check box. Unless the label is listening the click
> event and is able to check the check box (which of course is good
> implementation but don't work with all cases).


You don't click on the label. You use xpath to get the "for" attribute of
the label. This will return an element id of a form element. You then click
on that id. So, assuming you have something like "<label
for='id_username'>Username</label>", a locator of "label=Username" is
identical to the locator "id=id_username".

The benefit is that with "label=" you don't have to know the id of the form
element. All you need is to look at the web page and use the label.
Assuming the label doesn't change, the underlying id can change without
having to modify the test. This happens a lot with systems that
auto-generate ids.

For the RDA, the catalogs themselves do not require authentication. However, any service or html/xml page used by a service (like `dataset.xml` for `NCSS`) will require authentication. When you make a request to the server and the resource requires authentication, it will respond with a `HTTP 401 Unauthorized` and, included in those headers, will be something like: `WWW-Authenticate: Basic` For example: ~~~bash curl -i "https://rda.ucar.edu/thredds/dodsC/files/g/ds084.1/2015/20150116/gfs.0p25.2015011600.f006.grib2.ascii?LatLon_Projection" HTTP/1.1 401 Unauthorized Date: Mon, 31 Jul 2017 13:45:40 GMT Server: Apache/2.4.6 (CentOS) Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=63072000; includeSubdomains; preload Cache-Control: private Expires: Wed, 31 Dec 1969 17:00:00 MST WWW-Authenticate: Basic realm="THREDDS Data Server" Content-Language: en Content-Length: 994 Content-Type: text/html;charset=utf-8 ~~~ So, if you get a `401`, you check the `WWW-Authenticate` part of the header to see what type of authorization is required. Common possibilities include `Basic`, `Digest`, and `OAuth`, although there are [others](http://www.iana.org/assignments/http-authschemes/http-authschemes.xhtml). Once you get the `401`, it's up to the client to decide what to do. In toolsUI, a log-in gui widget will appear: but there are any number of ways of doing things (e.g. command line prompt, let user set credentials as part of Siphon API). Now, where this gets a little tricky is when Siphon piggybacks on another library for access, like how we use netCDF4-Python to speak OPeNDAP (which then relies on netCDF-C). The C library does [this](https://www.unidata.ucar.edu/software/netcdf/docs/auth.html) for auth. Pretty gnarly, but we might be able to get away with detecting the case of a `401` trying to access an OPeNDAP resource, and insert the credentials into the DAP URL (case 2 in the previously linked netCDF-C doc on authentication). However, if the group running the OPeNDAP enabled server has their own authentication service and it involves a redirect (like NASA URS), then things really get fun (see section 4 of the netCDF-C doc), but thankfully [NASA has some docs](https://wiki.earthdata.nasa.gov/display/EL/How+To+Access+Data+With+Python) to help guide us for that.

From PEP 508:
> The job of a dependency is to enable tools like pip [1] to find the right package to install. Sometimes this is very loose - just specifying a name, and sometimes very specific - referring to a specific file to install. Sometimes dependencies are only relevant in one platform, or only some versions are acceptable, so the language permits describing all these cases.

Given that the grammar explicitly includes multi-version and version comparison, I would go so far as to say Pipenv _does not support_ PEP 508 for Python version specification. Perhaps there should be a change to the documentation to avoid confusion with regard to this issue?

D'oh, we completely missed that `platform_machine` gave the wrong answer for 32-bit binaries running on 64-bit systems back when PEP 345 was defined (which is where the current environment marker set comes from).

@uranusjr's suggestion of defining a `platform_architecture_bits` marker would work, but I'm also inclined to ask whether it might make sense to work out a way to directly support PEP 425 compatibility tags in the environment marker syntax, so users don't need to learn two different ways of identifying platforms.

The downside of the latter option is that the way we spell the 32-bit vs 64-bit distinction in platform tags is unfortunately platform dependent (it's always `x86_64` on Mac OS X, it's `x86_64` vs `i686` on Linux, and then it's `win32` vs `win_amd64` on Windows).

Either way, PEP 508 would need a replacement PEP that gives it the same treatment that PEP 566 gave to the core metadata spec:

* make https://packaging.python.org/specifications/dependency-specifiers/ the reference document for the current dependency specifier format (rather than the PEP itself), such that future PEPs (including that one) only need to cover their additions, and not repeat the whole spec
* add any new environment markers that we think are missing (e.g. we may decide to add `platform_architecture_bits`)
* amend any environment markers that experience has shown are poorly defined (e.g. despite the name, it might be reasonable to decide that `platform_machine` should be checking against the compatible ABI of the running Python interpreter, not the bit-width of the underlying CPU)

I have thought about this quite a bit, so here's my proposal for how a change could be rolled out:

- 9.1:
   - Add `PIPENV_VENV_HOME`, which defaults to `~/.local/share/virtualenvs` (or equivalent)
  - Use `PIPENV_VENV_HOME` as the root directory for virtual environments unless `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` is set
- 9.2:
  - Add a depreciation warning about `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT` being removed in 10.0
  - Add a depreciation warning about needing to set a value for `PIPENV_VENV_HOME` in 10.0 to keep the current behavior
- 10.0:
  - Remove `PIPENV_VENV_IN_PROJECT`
  - No longer set a default value for `PIPENV_VENV_HOME`
  - Create virtual environments in the project root unless `PIPENV_VENV_HOME` is set

The issue is that I performed a manual release today, and I forgot to set an environment variable first. If SETUPTOOLS_INSTALL_WINDOWS_SPECIFIC_FILES is not set, and the release is cut from a non-Windows system, the files get omitted.

This situation is not suitable. Setuptools should not be defaulting to creating buggy builds. I'm going to change the behavior to release these files by default, and if an environment wants to make a build without these files, it must do so by disabling the behavior.

Prior to Setuptools 34, setuptools would vendor its dependencies, including them as hacked-in copies of the dependencies. Thus `import pkg_resources.extern.six` would give the vendored version of six if present as `pkg_resources._vendor.six` or would fall back to the top-level `six` otherwise. It would do this even when installed. In this model, the dependencies were undeclared (nothing in `install_requires`).

With this new proposed technique, the dependencies are bundled into the sdist only to facilitate building and installing from source... but the dependencies are still declared as `install_requires`, thus relying pip or easy_install or whatever install those requirements, but providing a zip of those dependencies to use to build the package prior to installation. In the installed environment, there are no import hacks and the packages must have been installed properly and naturally.

This behaviour appears only in Storm 0.9.4. Specifically, `[org.apache.storm/storm-core "0.9.2-incubating"]` does not exhibit this issue, but `[org.apache.storm/storm-core "0.9.4"]` does.

I was able to replicate this with the following simple topology derived from the bootstrap word-count example (only modified files shown below):

*src/bolts/wordcount.py* (replace Bolt with BatchingBolt)
```python
from __future__ import absolute_import, print_function, unicode_literals

from collections import Counter
from streamparse.bolt import BatchingBolt

class WordCounter(BatchingBolt):

    def initialize(self, conf, ctx):
        pass

    def process_batch(self, key, tups):
        for tup in tups:
            if tup.task < 0:
                self.log('BUG: ' + str(tup))
```

*project.clj* (replace Storm 0.9.2 with Storm 0.9.4)

```clojure
(defproject topo "0.0.1-SNAPSHOT"
  :source-paths ["topologies"]

  :target-path "_build"

```

Thanks for starting this discussion! In order to explicitly state the problem we are trying to solve, here is some more context:

For the go-faster project, a set of [kinto collections](http://kinto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/concepts.html#buckets-collections-and-records) will be used to publish updates to the User-Agents (Firefox instances) out in the wild.

This data is globally shared and all the client will only have read access to it. Rather than requesting the data to Kinto at each request, it might make sense to cache Kinto's response.

The clients, in order to retrieve the latest data, will issue a `GET /buckets/{bid}/collections/{cid}/records?_since={timestamp}` as described in the (polling for change section)[http://kinto.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/synchronisation.html#polling-for-changes] of the docs.

Each time the collection is updated, the cache should be invalidated, and eventually recreated. We could use the Kinto notification system in order to ping the caching layer.

With this in mind, I looked at the documents you pointed out Tarek (thanks!). I don't see any way to invalidate the cache in there, tough.

Before digging more in the details of caching, I think we should ensure a few things:

Do we need to invalidate the cache just after a database update? If not, we might just wait for a specific period and let the cache expire, and the origin server being called again;

Is NGINX the caching layer we will use in production? (hi @micheletto, @deanwilson)

As far as I can see the following is causing problems:
https://github.com/zopefoundation/Products.PythonScripts/blob/master/src/Products/PythonScripts/tests/testPythonScript.py#L62

So the tests of Products.PythonScripts use this `verify` on each script before they run it. Currently there is no `verify` available so the only suggestion I have is removing this line.

Let my try to explain why the `verify` was dropped and not ported.
The new implementation of RestrictedPython is not using bytecode transformations at all, only AST modifications. Implementing a good verify is not so trivial. Bytecode in python 2/3 differs a little in some places. Furthermore we have too keep up with changes to it with newer versions of python. 
The new unittests here (https://github.com/zopefoundation/RestrictedPython/tree/master/tests) are using a different approach to ensure that RestrictedPython does what it should. By really executing the code.
Will provide a more general solution.

It turns out that `AuthenticationFailed` [doesn't always raise a 401](http://www.django-rest-framework.org/api-guide/authentication/#unauthorized-and-forbidden-responses).  I removed `SessionAuthentication` and `BasicAuthentication` from my `DEFAULT_AUTHENTICATION_CLASSES` which fixes my issue.

> But generally, these formats seem extremely wasteful - you could probably get an order of magnitude better compression with a cheap but dedicated compressor.

Our data is representative of a lot of non-video large binary streams -- it is composed of long streams of floats and integers, exactly what should be in a binary data stream.  Our case is an easy one because they are even 32-bit boundary aligned.  Of course we could write a custom compressor but then it will be in JavaScript again and we lose the speed and memory benefits of having it integrated into the browser.  It would also take a lot of time for us and also force that on all other 3D developers -- thus it is not a globally efficient solution nor a locally efficient solution.

I would prefer to just use LZMA.js in that case or a browser-based compression tool that produces similar results.  Basically saying that I have to write a custom compression scheme because Brotli is inadequate compared to LZMA is not an acceptable answer.  We have good general purpose compression schemes for a reason and right now Brotli isn't one, even though it claims to be.

OK, I think I'm done futzing with this now.  I added a .coveragerc file to have it ignore various kinds of things that we don't actually care about in terms of unit test coverage.  And for some specific bits of code (special cases for old systems, obsolete code that's still sitting there, warnings about bad user input, etc.) I marked them with a `pragma: no cover` comment to exclude them from the coverage report.

I also added code to run the des and great3 examples, which covers a bit more of the code base, at least for syntax errors, if not actual correctness testing, although I think for the additional bits that these catch, syntax errors are probably the main thing we'd worry about.

After all that, the remaining bits I think are code that ought to be included in the unit tests, and of them, we're currently at 93% coverage.  Not too shabby, IMHO.  You can peruse them [here](https://coveralls.io/github/GalSim-developers/GalSim).  It's pretty cool -- the covered lines are green, and the uncovered lines are pink.

I even felt inspired to add a unit test for the `NFWHalo.getLensing` function that was completely untested.  And in doing so, I discovered a bug in it, so that was useful.  I also found a number of typos in uncovered bits of code (like writing `NotImplemented` rather than `NotImplementedError`), so I fixed them as well.

The Tan WCS type is possibly what you want.  It essentially lets you define the CD matrix and CRVALs (to use the language of the Griesen and Calabretta WCS parametrization).

If you want to read in a WCS from a FITS file and then change some of the parameters around, no that's not native to GalSim, but it wouldn't be too hard to write a custom WCS class that would do this for you.

cf. https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/wiki/Config-Image#wcs for instructions about how to do this.  (And about the Tan WCS type.)

For these half-complete features, I wanted to document what remains.

Remains to be done:

- Should have made `--source-vars` on group the `Variable` type as well.
- Allow multiple flags
- More detailed command testing
- Unit tests

The multiple flags feature is the real tricky one here. Click combines multiple flags into a tuple. I looked into it, and it would appear that all we need is to override `type_cast_value` on the Option class.

https://github.com/mitsuhiko/click/blob/6fcda3e5fcddd9001b314e83a1e0546bd22c78c2/click/core.py#L1319

Now, part of the trickery is that our custom types override `click.types.ParamType`, not `click.option`. Even click.option actually comes from a wrapper that lives at `click.decorators.option`. Mucking with click.option is still perfectly fine with me, and this is logical since the combination into a tuple occurs at a lower level than the parameter type definition. But this is still more ambitious than the norm.

Additionally, the ability to provide a custom `Option` class appears to be a specific [feature](https://github.com/mitsuhiko/click/commit/8e2e583694ac0b374a3aa9712e8274ef3519f1d1) of click. So the door is open to overwrite what I'm thinking about, I just have to make sure this is the best practice.

This doesn't look correct. `PyUnicode_AsUTF8String()` returns a `PyObject *`, not a C string (https://docs.python.org/3/c-api/unicode.html#c.PyUnicode_AsUTF8String), so you're casting an object to `(char *)` which is then treated as a C string by `forbidden_check()`. So then the latter will always return 1.

The original code used `PyBytes_AS_STRING()` which does return a C string.

> I find it somewhat annoying that certain operations (e.g, np.ma.sqrt) on a masked array can change its fill_value attribute without warning

This has actually been fixed in NumPy 1.11.0

``` Python
import numpy as np
print(np.__version__)
a = np.ma.arange(10, fill_value=88)
print(a.fill_value)
print(np.ma.sqrt(a).fill_value)
```

NumPy 1.11.0
```
1.11.0
88
88
```

Old broken behavior in NumPy 1.10.4
```
1.10.4
88
1e+20
```

Now I'm sure there are still more issues with fill_value but at least things are improving!

Hiragana output is currently supported with the `reading` flag, but I'm not sure if that's the best way to choose it if you want to choose output between multiple different writing systems. There's also katakana, which would be pretty easy to implement if I remember the Unicode code points correctly.

Romaji is a bit complicated, though. First, there are multiple ways to romanize Japanese text, as seen from your link. An optional dependency to something like [python-romkan](https://github.com/soimort/python-romkan) could be added, but even then there would be the problem of separating the number words with spaces only for romanized Japanese but not with kana. Quick update:
* Went digging further.
* Given my understanding for the need of connection-level authenticatin, suspected requests' bundled HTTPDigestAuth would be a good candidate to suffer from the same limitaions.
* Created a multi-threaded test client that shows this to be the case.
* Ended up finding requests' issue 2334 (https://github.com/kennethreitz/requests/issues/2334) with a sample test client: same failure, same underlying issue.

Extra:
* Found out requests bundled urllib3 (and urllib3 by itself) includes a contributed NTLMConnectionPool. This takes a different approach at addressing NTLM authentication.

Next:
* Regarding this PR: waiting on your feedback, of course.
* Regarding my need for multi-threaded NTLM authentication:
  * May give the NTLMConnectionPool a test run even though it does not integrate as nicely with my codebase, since it does not work at the requests/auth API level.
  * If my understanding is correct, the current requests authentication model, HTTPAdapter and the underlying urllib3's PoolManager design provide no way to ensure that a particular requests authenticator gets the same connection that got an initial 401 (unless pools block and have at most one connection, I believe). Fixing this while keeping backwards compatibility seems feasible, but is something non-immediate and, of course, outside of the scope of this PR and project. :)

The two errors have slightly different meanings.

`too much contention on these datastore entities` means one of the entity groups in the transaction changed after it was first read or modified.

`transaction closed` means a request contained a transaction that was already closed (e.g. because it was already committed, rolled back, or closed by the server because some earlier transactional operation failed).

Couple of questions to try to narrow this down:
